# Brief: David (PERSONA-002)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-03-19

## Since Last Meeting

No inter-meeting evidence arrived in this period. David enters March 19 with the same open ledger he carried out of the March 9 school board meeting.

The absence of news is itself a data point worth naming. Three items David flagged as time-sensitive remain unresolved: health insurance actuals are not due until March 25 — six days after this meeting — meaning the FY26 projection range ($170K surplus to $1M deficit) is still live. The no-reconfiguration alternative has not been modeled. Transportation and Pre-K mandate costs remain uncosted. None of those gaps close this week.

What this meeting offers is different: it is a city council meeting, not a school board meeting. David's running concern about institutionally isolated fiscal universes applies directly here. The March 19 agenda contains items that load directly onto the same property tax base as the school district's $8–9M gap — and at least one of them is finally forcing a decision the council has avoided for months.

---

## Open Questions

David carries these into the meeting:

- **Why hasn't anyone produced the combined property tax burden math?** Mahoney renovation ($57–104M range), existing city bonds ($89.1M), Pearl Street revenue bonds (~$50M), and the school district's structural gap ($8–9M) all hit the same residential base — 61% of which is already carried by homeowners. Staff documents still haven't put this on one page.
- **When does the Mahoney design team get a number?** The council has been asked for a cost ceiling since at least January. Every workshop ends with informal "directional guidance." What procedurally forces a binding vote tonight?
- **Is the sewer revenue bond on the agenda a new obligation I haven't been tracking — and what does it add to total debt service per household?**
- **Does the April 14 school budget workshop format actually allow for decision-making, or is it another presentation session?** The district has three workshops scheduled and a $3M+ unresolved gap. The calendar is compressing.
- **What are the actual vacancies being deferred on the appointments calendar — do any touch boards with fiscal or audit oversight roles?**
- **Will the council connect anything happening tonight to the school budget crisis, or will these two tracks continue running in parallel with no synthesis?**

---

## Agenda Implications

### H.1 — City Facilities: Mahoney Guidance

This is the meeting's most consequential item for David, and the one he should track with full attention.

The position paper asks the council three distinct questions: proceed with the scaled-down project or look at alternative City Hall locations; explore a public/private partnership via RFP at Mahoney; and how to address library facility needs. A supporting document ("260319 SoPo City Facilities Council Meeting Draft.pdf") has been posted.

**What this means for David:** This is the closest the council has come to making a formal decision on Mahoney since the architect surfaced the $57–104M renovation range in February. The RFP question in particular introduces a new variable: a public/private partnership process could delay the referendum timeline, shift the cost structure, or reframe the project in ways that affect its property tax footprint — none of which have been modeled against the school district's simultaneous gap.

**What to listen for:**
- Does the council vote on anything tonight, or does this again resolve as "guidance"? Watch for whether any council member calls for a formal vote on a cost ceiling versus simply taking a straw poll.
- If an RFP is authorized, what timeline does staff assign? A developer engagement process that runs through fall 2026 effectively pushes any referendum and its associated bond into a window when South Portland taxpayers are also absorbing school budget impacts.
- Does any council member explicitly name the combined tax burden or reference the school budget situation? Or do Mahoney and the school gap continue to be discussed as separate fiscal realities?
- Does the presentation include an updated cost range, or is the $57–104M figure still the operative frame? If the range has tightened, that matters for referendum viability.

**Questions to prepare:**
- Is the $57–104M range still the live estimate, or has the design work at 5% completion produced a narrower figure?
- If the council opts for the RFP path, what is the timeline, and at what design phase does sunk cost exposure for the renovation option become material?
- Has staff produced any combined debt service projection that includes school budget impacts alongside Mahoney, Pearl Street, and existing city bonds?

---

### C.2 — Sewer Revenue Bond (Finance Director Sanborn)

The position paper includes a comparative sewer cost table showing South Portland at approximately $56/month ($670/year) — toward the lower end among comparable communities. A finance director memo accompanies the item.

**What this means for David:** This is a new debt instrument on the agenda. Revenue bonds are technically separate from general obligation bonds in that they are funded by user fees rather than property tax — but they still represent a ratepayer obligation and belong in the full fiscal picture David has been assembling. He has been tracking Pearl Street revenue bonds (~$50M) as part of the combined debt picture; this appears to be a different, potentially smaller instrument, but the amount has not been specified in the agenda text.

**What to listen for:**
- The bond amount and term. The memo title references Finance Director Sanborn — this is the same finance director whose hire David flagged as a potential stabilizing development. How this memo is structured will tell David something about Sanborn's analytical approach.
- Whether this bond is already reflected in current sewer rate projections or whether rates will need to increase.
- Whether council members ask about debt service coverage ratios — a basic fiscal soundness indicator that David would expect a competent finance team to include.

**Questions to prepare:**
- What is the bond amount, term, and annual debt service?
- Is this bond already embedded in the sewer rate comparison table, or would it require a rate increase beyond the figures shown?
- Has any staff document integrated this obligation into a full municipal debt service summary?

---

### E.5 — Budget Workshop Calendar (Order #161)

The position paper establishes the public hearing date and, embedded in its text, confirms the sequence for the two April budget workshops:

- **April 14, 2026 — Budget Workshop #1:** School listed first, followed by City Clerk, HR/Benefits, Social Services, Police, Fire, Library, Sustainability, Public Works, Facilities, Parks/Rec.
- **April 28, 2026 — Budget Workshop #2:** Administrative and support departments.

**What this means for David:** The school budget is the lead item on April 14. That workshop is 26 days away. The district's three currently scheduled reconfiguration workshops need to reach resolvable conclusions before April 14 if the school presentation is to include any firm scenario for the council to react to. If reconfiguration is still unsettled when April 14 arrives, the council will be asked to evaluate a budget with a structurally open $3M+ hole — which is precisely the dynamic David has been watching for since February.

**What to listen for:**
- Does any council member ask tonight what state the school reconfiguration decision will be in by April 14?
- Is there any mention of a joint workshop between city and school board before April 14, or do the two bodies arrive independently?
- Does the council's adoption of this schedule implicitly signal their expectation of what the school board will present — and is that expectation realistic given where reconfiguration stands?

**Questions to prepare:**
- Will the April 14 school presentation include a resolved reconfiguration scenario, or will the council be evaluating an incomplete budget?
- Has any staff document synchronized the school board's workshop timeline with the city's April 14 deadline?

Does any council member engage substantively, ask a follow-up, or note the April 14 deadline? Or does it receive the 90-second treatment it got in December?

Three people resigned from the same boards within a five-day window in January — that is a pattern David would want to understand before April 7. ## Watch For

- **Does the Mahoney discussion end with a vote or with guidance?** David has watched the council produce informal directional consensus three times without a binding ceiling. The position paper's three explicit questions are the clearest forcing mechanism yet. Watch for whether any council member calls the question or whether the discussion drifts into a fourth deferral.

- **Whether the RFP path gets seriously debated** — and if so, whether any council member models the timeline implications. An RFP process that runs six months pushes any Mahoney referendum ballot into 2027 and overlaps with the full ramp of school budget cuts hitting residential property owners.

- **Finance Director Sanborn's presentation style on the sewer bond.** This is David's first opportunity to watch Sanborn operate in a public city council setting on a financial item. Does the memo include debt service analysis? Coverage ratios? Year-over-year comparison? The quality of this presentation is a data point on whether Prince's confidence in the new finance director is warranted — or whether personnel optimism is still substituting for structural controls.

- **Whether any council member names the school budget while discussing Mahoney.** If someone on the council connects tonight's facility discussion to the April 14 school budget deadline, that is the first sign the two fiscal universes are beginning to integrate. If no one does, the structural isolation David identified in December is still operating.

- **Whether the April 14 budget workshop structure gets any discussion.** The school is listed first, but nothing in the agenda text specifies what the school board will present or whether it will include a resolved reconfiguration scenario. If a council member asks about this, track both the question and the answer.

- **The three January vacancies** (Springer, Maxfield, Duvernay — all resigned within a five-day window). The appointments calendar defers this to April 7 without explanation. David should note which boards these seats belong to and whether any carry fiscal, audit, or TIF oversight responsibility. Clustering of resignations from the same body in the same week is a flag worth investigating before the April 7 meeting.

- **Whether anyone mentions a combined debt service figure** — any synthesis of Mahoney, Pearl Street, sewer bond, existing city bonds, and school district gap against a single property tax base. If that number surfaces anywhere in tonight's discussion, it matters more than any individual line item on the agenda.

- **Council tone on the scaled-down Mahoney scenario versus the RFP path.** These are different fiscal risk profiles. Scaled-down renovation has a known (if wide) cost range and can proceed to referendum on a defined timeline. The RFP path is open-ended and carries sunk-cost exposure if the design advances and a private partner doesn't materialize. Watch for which option gets more substantive engagement versus performative interest.
